generated_oml:
// ---------- Sensing Components ----------
instance InternalTempSensorA : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Internal temperature sensor #1 inside the incubator box"
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTempDataTxA
]

instance InternalTempSensorB : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Internal temperature sensor #2 inside the incubator box"
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTempDataTxB
]

instance ExternalTempSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "External temperature sensor measuring room temperature"
    DTDFVocab:producedData ExternalTempDataTx
]

// ---------- Data Transmitted ----------
instance InternalTempDataTxA : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTempSensorA
]

instance InternalTempDataTxB : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTempSensorB
]

instance ExternalTempDataTx : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ExternalTempSensor
]

instance AverageBoxTempDataTx : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTempSensorA, InternalTempSensorB
]

// ---------- Data ----------
instance InternalTempDataA : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Raw temperature reading (°C) from internal sensor A"
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTempDataTxA
    DTDFVocab:inputTo LowLevelDriver
]

instance InternalTempDataB : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Raw temperature reading (°C) from internal sensor B"
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTempDataTxB
    DTDFVocab:inputTo LowLevelDriver
]

instance ExternalTempData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Raw temperature reading (°C) from external sensor"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ExternalTempDataTx
    DTDFVocab:inputTo LowLevelDriver
]

instance AverageBoxTempData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Computed average box temperature from the two internal sensors"
    DTDFVocab:fromData AverageBoxTempDataTx
    DTDFVocab:inputTo LowLevelDriver
]

// ---------- Acting Components ----------
instance HeatBedActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Resistive heat‑bed heater providing thermal energy to the incubator"
]

instance FanActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Internal fan that circulates air for uniform temperature distribution"
]

// ---------- Enablers ----------
instance LowLevelDriver : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Low‑level driver that abstracts I/O, enforces safety protocol and publishes sensor data via RabbitMQ"
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeVisualizationService,
                     CalibrationService,
                     AnomalyDetectionService,
                     HeatBedStateEstimationService,
                     WhatIfSimulationService,
                     SelfAdaptationService
]

instance RabbitMQBroker : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "RabbitMQ message broker using AMQP for communication between driver, controller and DT services"
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeVisualizationService,
                     CalibrationService,
                     AnomalyDetectionService,
                     HeatBedStateEstimationService,
                     WhatIfSimulationService,
                     SelfAdaptationService
]

instance TimeSeriesDatabase : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB) that stores incoming temperature streams for dashboards and analysis"
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeVisualizationService,
                     CalibrationService,
                     AnomalyDetectionService,
                     HeatBedStateEstimationService
]

instance CalibrationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Calibration routine that adjusts free parameters of thermal models using logged sensor data"
    DTDFVocab:enables CalibrationService
]

instance KalmanFilterEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with sensor data for heat‑bed state estimation and anomaly detection"
    DTDFVocab:enables AnomalyDetectionService,
                     HeatBedStateEstimationService
]

instance CoSimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Co‑simulation engine that runs what‑if scenarios using historical or live PT data"
    DTDFVocab:enables WhatIfSimulationService
]

instance SelfAdaptationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Self‑adaptation loop that orchestrates anomaly detection, experiment scheduling, re‑calibration and controller re‑deployment"
    DTDFVocab:enables SelfAdaptationService
]

// ---------- Models ----------
instance ThermalModelA : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Analytical thermal model A with two free parameters based on basic heat equation and four simplifying assumptions"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo CalibrationEngine, KalmanFilterEngine
]

instance ThermalModelB : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Analytical thermal model B extending Model A by relaxing the power‑conversion assumption and adding two extra free parameters"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo CalibrationEngine, KalmanFilterEngine, CoSimulationEngine
]

// ---------- Services ----------
instance RealTimeVisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real‑time visualization of incubator status by ingesting sensor streams, storing them, and displaying on an interactive dashboard"
    DTDFVocab:provides TemperatureVisualizationInsight
]

instance CalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "On‑demand calibration of thermal models using the logged temperature time series"
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelCalibrationInsight,
                     UpdateModelParametersAction
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Anomaly detection using a Kalman filter that compares Model B predictions with measured data"
    DTDFVocab:provides TemperatureAnomalyInsight
]

instance HeatBedStateEstimationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Estimation of the heat‑bed temperature state via Kalman filtering"
    DTDFVocab:provides HeatBedStateInsight
]

instance WhatIfSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "What‑if simulation through co‑simulation of historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller parameters"
    DTDFVocab:provides OptimizationInsight
]

instance SelfAdaptationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Self‑adaptation loop that closes the DT‑PT feedback cycle: detect anomalies, schedule experiments, recalibrate, reconfigure, and redeploy controller"
    DTDFVocab:provides ReconfigureControllerAction,
                     DeployUpdatedControllerAction
]

// ---------- Insights ----------
instance TemperatureVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight showing current temperature trends and status on the dashboard"
]

instance ModelCalibrationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight reporting calibration error metrics and updated model parameter values"
]

instance TemperatureAnomalyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight flagging deviations between measured temperatures and Model B predictions"
]

instance HeatBedStateInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight estimating unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and its confidence interval"
]

instance OptimizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Insight delivering optimal controller parameter sets derived from what‑if simulations"
]

// ---------- Actions ----------
instance UpdateModelParametersAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatically updates the free parameters of the selected thermal model after calibration"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ReconfigureControllerAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatically reconfigures the bang‑bang controller thresholds based on detected anomalies"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance DeployUpdatedControllerAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Deploys the newly configured controller software onto the Raspberry Pi"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// ---------- Environment ----------
instance IncubatorEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains HeatBedActuator, FanActuator, InternalTempSensorA, InternalTempSensorB, ExternalTempSensor
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "Control commands generated by the digital twin or the controller are sent as messages over RabbitMQ to the low‑level driver, which then actuates the physical twin by turning the heat‑bed on/off, setting heating duration (H), waiting duration (C), and controlling the fan. The low‑level driver validates received commands through its built‑in safe protocol before execution, and the controller also publishes its current control state back to RabbitMQ, providing implicit confirmation of command execution."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensors and publishes the measurements to the RabbitMQ communication server; the controller on the Raspberry Pi subscribes to the same broker, receives commands and publishes its state. All data flow is asynchronous but designed for near‑real‑time operation, supporting real‑time visualization and on‑demand calibration. The exact sampling period is not specified, but the architecture relies on periodic sensor acquisition and low‑latency message passing between driver, controller and DT components."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin is orchestrated around a RabbitMQ server that acts as the central communication hub for all components. The low‑level driver publishes sensor readings, the controller subscribes to receive commands and publishes its state, and DT services (calibration algorithms, safety monitors, Kalman‑filter based state estimator, anomaly detection, what‑if simulation, and self‑adaptation loop) listen to the same message streams. Calibration algorithms continuously update model parameters, monitors evaluate safety and performance, and, upon detection of anomalies, the DT can trigger re‑calibration, re‑configure the controller, and launch simulation‑based optimisation, closing the loop between physical and cyber realms."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The DT lifecycle starts with a modelling phase where abstract CAD‑type and dynamic differential‑equation models of the incubator are created (Document 1). In the implementation phase the two simulation models (Model A and Model B) are coded and stored in the open‑source repository (Document 3, 5). The integration phase embeds the models, calibration algorithms and monitoring components into the DT architecture (Fig. 1, Document 1) and connects them to the physical twin via the low‑level driver and RabbitMQ communication server (Document 5). During the operational phase the DT continuously acquires sensor data (three temperature sensors) (Document 2) and runs monitors, fault detectors and optimisers; violations trigger human or automated actions. A maintenance/adaptation phase repeatedly executes on‑demand calibration, re‑estimates model parameters and updates the controller configuration, closing the self‑adaptation loop (Document 4). Transitions between phases are managed by calibration updates and by the monitor‑driven feedback, while governance is realized by continuous monitoring for safety and optimality."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "Model fidelity is based on four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature inside the box (enforced by the fan), (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls, (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air, and (4) all electrical power is converted to heat in the air (Model A) or, for Model B, the heat‑bed inertia is also modeled. Validation of the models is performed by a calibration step that adjusts model parameters to minimise the discrepancy between simulated temperature and the sensor measurements; the calibration algorithm is described as a calibration process. Uncertainty arises from the simplifying assumptions, sensor measurement errors, and any unmodeled dynamics. No quantitative uncertainty quantification or error‑correction mechanisms are detailed in the documents."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "All components of the incubator digital twin (controller, low‑level driver, and DT analytics) communicate through a RabbitMQ message broker. Each component connects as a client to the broker, using RabbitMQ’s messaging facilities to exchange sensor data and control commands. Specific bandwidth, latency, or redundancy requirements are not detailed in the documents, but RabbitMQ’s mature and reliable messaging infrastructure is employed."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The controller and the low‑level driver run on a Raspberry Pi that hosts the physical twin, while the RabbitMQ server is deployed on‑premise (same or nearby hardware); the deployment thus relies on on‑site hardware with modest computational and storage needs, and no cloud or edge platform is mentioned."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "Integration with external systems is achieved through the RabbitMQ message broker (using AMQP) which relays data between the incubator’s controller, low‑level driver and any DT components. DT components can forward the streamed data to a time‑series database for persistent storage and connect a dashboard for real‑time visualisation of temperature and control status. Thus, the DT interacts with external storage and visualisation tools via standard message‑based interfaces."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The DT for the incubator embeds safety mechanisms through a low‑level driver that incorporates a safe protocol; this driver periodically reads temperature sensors, validates readings, and ensures that commands sent to the heatbed and fan keep the physical twin within safe temperature bounds. The digital twin also runs monitors that evaluate real‑time data to detect unsafe or sub‑optimal behavior and can trigger corrective actions. However, the provided documents do not specify any cybersecurity protocols, authentication systems, access‑control mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, or formal risk‑assessment procedures."
]
